steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage instructs historians: when a story has ‘sufficient authority,’ name who vouched for it; when it lacks authority, say so explicitly. The core duty is transparency about how trustworthy each source is.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Historians rarely have access to all the information relevant to a particular event, so no single work of history should be taken as definitive.: the passage is about credibility signaling, not completeness. Different point. ✗
  • Choice B — The only figures that a historian should quote are those who are widely viewed as credible.: contradicts the passage, which says historians should include such accounts while flagging their low authority. ✗
  • Choice C — Historians should clearly indicate the extent to which each of their sources is trustworthy.: matches the passage's core instruction exactly. ✓
  • Choice D — Because historians cannot tell whose memories they should trust, they should avoid relying on eyewitness accounts of events.: the passage never says this. Unsupported claim. ✗
That matches Choice C — Historians should clearly indicate the extent to which each of their sources is trustworthy..
